note_data.title: Adjustments to financial statements
keyPoints: Financial statements provide information about financial position, financial performance and cash flows useful to users in making economic decisions
Manufacturing account is prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
Manufacturing account only includes information about the factory and actual manufacturing process
Non-production costs (administration, finance, distribution) are recorded in the Income Statement
Cost of production = prime cost + factory overheads
Transfer price = production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
Manufacturing profit is the difference between transfer price and production cost of completed goods

definitions.definition: An account prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
The total of direct materials, direct labour, and direct expenses
Costs incurred from the running of the factory, including indirect factory wages and depreciation of factory machinery
Inventory of partly finished goods in the factory at any point in time
Percentage added to the factory cost of production to arrive at the transfer price
The production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
The difference between the transfer price and the production cost of completed goods

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all working clearly. Apply the correct formula and state the final answer.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'. Reference the matching principle.
Use correct format with debit and credit columns. Include all necessary adjustments.

modelAnswer.question: A business purchased machinery for Rs 50,000 on 1 January 2023. The useful life is 5 years with a residual value of Rs 5,000. Calculate the annual depreciation charge using the straight-line method and explain why depreciation is charged.
modelAnswer.answer: Annual depreciation = (Rs 50,000 - Rs 5,000) ÷ 5 years = Rs 9,000 per year. Depreciation is charged because it follows the matching principle, ensuring that the cost of the asset is allocated over the periods in which it generates revenue. This provides a more accurate measurement of profit for each accounting period.
markBreakdown: Mark 4: Explanation of accurate profit measurement

commonMistakes: Including non-production costs like administration and finance costs in the manufacturing account instead of the Income Statement
Confusing prime cost with total production cost - prime cost only includes direct costs
Not understanding that manufacturing account only covers factory operations, not the entire business
examinerTips: Remember the formula: Cost of production = prime cost + factory overheads
Clearly separate manufacturing costs from other business costs when preparing financial statements
formulas: Straight-line depreciation = (Cost - Residual value) ÷ Useful life
Reducing balance depreciation = Carrying amount × Depreciation rate%
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-account showing the double entry for depreciation: Debit Depreciation expense (Income Statement), Credit Accumulated depreciation (Statement of Financial Position)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare adjusted trial balance after considering accruals, prepayments and depreciation for a manufacturing business
O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — candidates had to calculate bad debts provision and show the adjustment in both ledger accounts and final accounts
M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (10 marks) — question required students to prepare trading and profit & loss account with adjustments for closing stock, insurance prepaid, and rent owing
O/N 2023 P2 Q2 (4 marks) — focused specifically on depreciation adjustments using both straight-line and reducing balance methods

workedExample.question: The following trial balance was extracted from the books of Ahmed Trading Co. on 31 December 2023. Additional information: (i) Rent paid in advance $200 (ii) Electricity owing $150 (iii) Depreciation on equipment 10% per annum using straight-line method. Equipment cost $5,000. Prepare the adjusted trial balance. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the adjustments needed - prepaid rent (+$200 asset, -$200 expense), accrued electricity (+$150 liability, +$150 expense), depreciation (+$500 expense, +$500 accumulated depreciation)
Step 3: Adjust rent account - reduce rent expense by $200, create prepaid rent (current asset) $200
Step 4: Adjust electricity - increase electricity expense by $150, create accrued electricity (current liability) $150
Step 5: Record depreciation - create depreciation expense $500, create accumulated depreciation (contra asset) $500
Step 6: Prepare adjusted trial balance ensuring debits = credits after all adjustments
workedExample.answer: Adjusted Trial Balance shows: Prepaid rent $200 (Dr), Accrued electricity $150 (Cr), Depreciation expense $500 (Dr), Accumulated depreciation $500 (Cr), with corresponding reductions/increases to original rent and electricity expenses
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for correct prepaid rent adjustment, 2 marks for accrued electricity, 2 marks for depreciation calculation and recording, 2 marks for proper presentation of adjusted trial balance

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to distinguish between accruals and prepayments, often adding when they should subtract' — always remember prepayments reduce expenses while accruals increase expenses

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to show both sides of adjustment entries' — every adjustment must have equal debit and credit effects, show both the balance sheet and income statement impacts
Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak performance in depreciation adjustments, many used wrong formulas' — always check if depreciation is on cost or net book value, and whether it's for full year or part year
note_data.keyDiagram: Create a T-account adjustment diagram showing: Original Trial Balance (left side) → Adjustment Entries (middle) → Adjusted Trial Balance (right side). Include arrows showing how each adjustment affects both an income statement item and balance sheet item. For example: Prepaid Insurance arrow shows reduction in Insurance Expense (P&L) and creation of Prepaid Insurance asset (Balance Sheet). This visual helps students see the dual effect of every adjustment entry.
note_data.quickRecap: Manufacturing accounts calculate production costs by adding factory overheads to prime costs. They only include factory-related costs, while other business expenses go to the Income Statement. Transfer pricing involves adding a mark-up to production costs, creating manufacturing profit.
